replies.title: Re: MOE Healing and omens of salted pork
replies.content:
1) I just had a cold after an intense trip to Guadalajara. Started with itchy throat and runny nose. In the middle of a class I was taking fever started to come. I gave myself healing hands. Went away. 2 hours later it came again, so I did it again and it went away again. This happened 3-4 times so I figured I had to give myself healing hands every two hours, so I did. It's the fastest I've healed from a cold. The effect was almost instant. I don’t happen to know if the fever went away or just the feeling like crap went away but certainly something did. One thing I noticed is that general inflammation would reduce noticeably. Ears would pop open and nose would unstuff. Every time after healing hands more mucus would come out of nowhere, even if I had just blown my nose. I am very febrile, I go to 39C easily. This time it never went above 38C. I thought that was cool. Kept it every 2 hours according to symptoms. Then every 6, then every 8 until I got back to my default once a day.

The only scenario where I have found caution to be needed is if someone's nervous system is sensitive or overwhelmed, in particular woman. For example, someone going through frequent panic attacks, anxiety, irritability, emotional sensitivity, mood swings and the likes. The person I did HH to in this condition reported a sensation of heat inside her body for 10 minutes that was uncomfortable, but never painful and then proceeded to have the most restful sleep she'd had in months. I asked her where she felt the heat. It was in the legs and it felt like it was moving up and down, around the knees. She has autism, a lot of people with autism have "restless leg syndrome" and energetically that means that there is a flow problem with the exchange of energy with the earth through the feet. I know because I have it and have noticed it in my magic practices. The way I get rid of it is by pushing the energy down and out through my feet for 5 minutes. So I did the same, I used my hand aided by visualization to move out the energy down and out through her feet. She felt instant relief and slept in the middle of it. After this happened I instructed her to do some deep breathing and stretching before the HH session and it never happened again but she still got very sleepy after the sessions, which is usual in people with anxiety when the anxiety goes away. She kept asking for it, which means it was helping.

The other caution I've seen is that since it helps the natural healing capabilities of the body, things like runny noses, diarrhea, or sweating might happen, so its a good idea to warn the consultant that it might happen and that it is a good thing. In my experience it never last longs and is accompanied by a feeling of relief more than sickness.

Anyway, I have extended myself, but there you go, some healing hands notes for you. :-)

comments.title: Hoodoo
comments.content:
Which Hoodoo working do you prefer for attracting large sums of money without relying on a family member passing away and leaving you an inheritance?

Last week there was a discussion here about a massive Hoodoo book—around 4,700 pages—but I forgot the title. Is that book actually practical and useful from a hands-on perspective?

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo
replies.content:
1) I don't do workings of that kind. Workings that attempt to get money without earning it aren't quite as predictably disastrous as love workings aimed at a specific person, but I have yet to see someone have good results from such a working. Very often what happens is that you lose some substantial amount of money or valuables, via theft, accident, or what have you. Remember that for you to get money without earning it, someone else has to earn the money and then not get it; thus you're trying to increase the total amount of economic injustice in the world, and that's very likely to blow back on you good and hard.

If you want to do a working to earn more money, especially if you want to wake yourself up to opportunities to make money, that's quite another matter -- I've done very successful hoodoo workings for that using a green candle dressed with crown of success oil and magnet oil, and a spell paper under the candle five-spotted with those same oils. Start it on a Friday during the day and hour of Venus.

2) Sure, if you're willing to put in the time to study it. The collection (which you can find here contains well over 13,000 spells; if you can't learn something from it, you're not trying. It consists almost entirely of interviews with professional hoodoo doctors and rootworkers in the South and Midwest.

I've used the latter for some bursts and I agree that unless there is some avenue for the money to flow in, it will not work. A job, gig or some stream of revenue will open.

But god damn it, I can't help but wonder if using those books to ask for wisdom to find new gigs would be better than asking for money out of the blue because that wisdom could lead to any sort of remote work that the person may want to do to earn this annoying little thing people call 'money' lol

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo
replies.content: Bingo. Even in an economy as crazy as the one we're stuck with now, it's easier to find ways to make money than it is to get money without earning it. In my experience, the most powerful money spells are those that focus on becoming aware of opportunities to make money -- do that sort of working, and you'll be astonished by how many possibilities you notice that you never saw before, and nobody else sees at all. The most recent time I did this I stumbled fairly quickly into a $36K/year side gig, providing a certain service to a fringe market that next to nobody else seems to have noticed. You can do the same thing, so long as you're willing to provide some good or service that people want or need!

replies.title: Re: Hoodoo
replies.content:
Sure. Get a green candle -- I recommend novena candles, the kind that come in tall narrow glass jars. Get a small bottle of Crown of Success oil and Magnet oil (this latter is sometimes called Drawing oil), which you can find from any hoodoo supply store. (I get mine from Lucky Mojo Curio Co., if that's helpful.) Also get a brown paper bag and a pen.

Start this spell on a Friday, in the hour of Venus. (You can look this up in planetary hours calculators online.) The candle should go on a metal or ceramic tray in a place where it can burn safely for a week or so.

Tear -- don't cut -- a square of paper from the bag, 4 inches square approximately. All four edges must be torn. Write your name on the paper seven times, one above the other. Then rotate the paper 90 degrees and write "I see opportunities to make money" seven times, one above the other. It ends up looking a little like this:


Now use both oils, one at a time, to put a "five spot" of oil drops on the paper -- that means one drop in each corner and one in the center, like the five side of dice. Then fold the paper in half, folding it toward you; turn 90 degrees, and fold it in half again, folding it toward you. Put that paper underneath the candle.

Put five more drops of each oil into the candle. Light the candle, and say seven times, "I see good opportunities to make money everywhere I look." Then just let it burn.

Now comes the crucial point. As soon as you start noticing opportunities to make money -- and you will -- do something about them. Don't just sit on your rump. Choose one or more of the opportunities, do whatever research or preparation you need to make use of it, and do it. This is essential. Magic has to be grounded out in activity on the material plane or it will not function. Follow through, and you'll be on your way to prosperity and success.

comments.title: About praying for the dead
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

(I'm hoping that I didn't already ask you this question ... I've become very absentminded lately)

I recently found out on Facebook that a former classmate passed away about 8 years ago. It's my belief that I had missed the e-mail notification about her passing, and until recently, I had not looked at her FB page in at least a decade. Although we were not particularly close, (we were at best friendly acquaintances), I was still sad when I learned of her passing.

In addition to feeling awful that 1) she died prematurely and 2) I only found out about this 8 years after the fact, I also prayed for her soul. She was also Catholic like me; however, I am still admittedly in need of remedial re-education in my faith that can't be learned via pop culture.

That got me thinking: unless I'm mistaken, it was my impression from past MMs that prayers for the dead were “most effective” while the soul was shedding its etheric and astral bodies.

Am I mistaken about this?

I am assuming that even after these bodies have been shed, prayer is still "effective," but perhaps qualitatively different?

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: About praying for the dead
replies.content: Prayer is very helpful for the dead as long as they are between lives. While it has important benefits to souls that are shedding their etheric and astral bodies, it also helps souls who are processing the memories of the life just ended -- this experience is "hell," "purgatory," or "heaven," depending on what kind of choices the soul made during that life -- and has another set of benefits to souls who are preparing to reincarnate. These days, with fertility rates dropping sharply worldwide and births peaking or declining in most parts of the world, it's likely that your friend is still between lives and so will benefit considerably from your prayers.

replies.title: Re: About praying for the dead
replies.content: Is there a time limit?  My father, for example, died 45 years ago.

replies.title: Re: About praying for the dead
replies.content: He's almost certainly gone onto another incarnation by now. Still, you can do a divination and see if it's still appropriate.

replies.title: Re: About praying for the dead
replies.content: Is there harm in it if the person has already "gone on"?  Or is it just a safe bet to stick with those who are "recently" deceased?

replies.title: Re: About praying for the dead
replies.content: No, there's no harm in it, but it's like sending a letter to someone who moved so long ago that the old address no longer forwards to the new one.

replies.title: Re: About praying for the dead
replies.content:

Several Magic Mondays ago, I mentioned my paying respects to the grave of a music artist I'm a fan of, whose incarnation violently ended around 1999 or so. You assured me that such actions are meritorious, yet there's no way I can know whether they're in another life or not. Is paying such respects justified either way?
Also, what about a historical personality you respect who lived, say, a few centuries ago? Are they completely out of reach?

replies.title: Re: About praying for the dead
replies.content: Oh, you can always honor them. With someone more than a couple of centuries back, too, it's quite possible that they're permanently out of incarnation, in which case you may get a response...

comments.title: Rose Geranium
comments.content:
Hi Everyone,
A number of years ago, I was caught in a gossip storm on my little island and I felt very uncomfortable. I was having a magic bath of cold water and felt like putting some rose geranium oil in the bath. I felt so good after that bath, as if it had washed off all the hexes off me. I felt very clean and happy in moments from feeling very much the reverse.

Since then, I have used rose geranium oil to refresh myself and lift my spirits with very good success. A girlfriend gave me some rose geranium plants and they grew like mad on my porch. They smell lovely and I used them in cleansing, protection and comforting spells with good success.

I looked up the folkloric uses of this plant in Southern Africa where they come from and people there like to grow hedges of these scented plants around their houses to keep bad things away. Bad things do not like good things.

I also made a tincture of the leaves and added a little honey into the tincture and it tuned out to be one of the nicest liqueurs I have made. It also seems to be very helpful for the treatment of headaches and low spirits.

The plants themselves have typically shaped geranium leaves and tiny pink flowers. They leaves contain the glorious fragrance. If you can get this plant for your garden or house, I highly recommend you try it.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Rose Geranium
replies.content: Thank you for this. It's from experiential reports like this that the body of magical knowledge grows.

replies.title: Re: Rose Geranium
replies.content:
I recall some undergrads, back when I was in grad school, setting up an experiment testing different methods of repelling ticks. It was a pretty well designed little study that included DEET, some other commercial insect repellants and rose geranium oil. They found that the DEET and the rose geranium had similar high efficacy in repelling ticks and all the other products didn't do much.

I'm a ways south of you but ticks (and opossums which I don't mind, unlike the ticks) are becoming more prevalent. Never hurts to have a natural tool to deter them!

HV

replies.title: Re: Rose Geranium
replies.content: When my children went to elementary school in rural Chile, we used to spray a lice repellent on them before school every morning. It was made of rose geranium oil. The smell confused the lice into thinking my children weren't going to be a good meal.  They never got lice.

Max

comments.title: Christian banishing rituals
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

While reading your October 2025 "Fifth Wednesday" post "A Few Notes on Psychic Self-Defense," I had misread what you had written about the Rosary:

"Any form of meditation that involves concentrating on a single thought or a sequence of thoughts will do the job. Five minutes a day is enough; thirty minutes a day is too much for most people. ... (Oh, and if you’re Catholic and aren’t yet praying the Rosary daily, get off your rump and on your knees. The Rosary is, among other things, a fine method of meditation.)"

In misreading and misremembering what you had written, my mind thought you had classified the Rosary as a banishing ritual.

After realizing my mistake, I began to wonder if there are any Christian prayers that are actually bona fide banishing rituals, yet were somehow saved from the Inquisition or other movements to purge Christianity of as many of its occult-like elements as possible. I know that you aren't Christian, but I am curious if you, or any Christian occultists you know, have any information on this.

Additionally, it is my understanding from your Dreamwidth posts that the Sphere of Protection can be tailored to Catholic or other Christian sensibilities. I'm assuming the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram cannot be so tailored?

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: Christian banishing rituals
replies.content:
1) I hope Robert Mathiesen comments on this -- he knows of a whole flurry of late medieval and Renaissance books of prayers that were used to banish evil spirits in the name of Christ. I've lost the references, but they're free for the downloading on Archive.org.

2) The Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram is a Christian ritual. It begins and ends with the sign of the Cross, invokes God by his Hebrew names, and calls on the four archangels. About the only way you could make it more Christian is to replace the Hebrew names with ישוה, the Hebrew spelling of Jesus (Yod, Shin, Vav, Heh).

replies.title: Re: Christian banishing rituals
replies.content:
If I may add a comment - most Catholic practices around protection form evil are not banishing rituals per se, but they fill a similar role. They come in three categories:

One is asking for aid - for this, the St Michael prayer (customarily said at the end of the Rosary, and many other times) is very commonly used. "St Michael the Archangel, defend us in battle! Be our protection against the wickedness and snares of the devil. May God rebuke him, we humbly pray, and do thou, O Prince of the Heavenly Host, cast into hell Satan, and all evil spirits who prowl about the world seeking the ruin of souls."

The second is using sacramentals - especially Blessed Salt and Holy Water, particularly the "Epiphany Water" blessed on the Feast of the Epiphany, and some of the medals (especially St Benedict and St Michael, and if you're thinking "that sounds like an amulet" I won't argue).

The third is prayers specifically for protection - St Patrick's Breastplate is well-known for this purpose, and I'd put Psalm 91 (mentioned above) in this category as well.

Catholic teaching tends toward "leave direct confrontation to those more powerful than you" - there are a lot of prayers and exorcisms, but mostly they're used by people with special roles vs normal laity. (I'm not expressing a "this is a good practice", just "this is a typical practice".)

SamChevre

comments.title: About affirmations

Hi JMG,

In re-reading your 08-30-2019 Dreamwidth post “Affirmations: An Introduction” (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/64539.html), it dawned on me that this was one magical practice that I often did in the past, when I regularly attended Co-Dependents Anonymous meetings.

Now, I admittedly wish that you could review the 35 affirmations listed on CODA’s website (https://coda.org/affirmations/helpful-affirmations/), candidly confessing that my self-esteem is so low that I can’t fully trust myself to evaluate correctly each affirmation according to your Seven Laws of Affirmation.

But I am hoping you will indulge me by reviewing my evaluation of one affirmation listed on CODA’s website according to your Seven Laws: “10. I am grateful for what I have and who I am.”

1. I am not trying to get the world to cater to me. Having been so ungrateful has prevented me from taking full advantage of opportunities in the past because I underestimated what I had and therefore also overestimated what I lacked. OK

2. “Avoid the language of entitlement. ... Choose something that motivates you to act and change, not something that inflates your ego and encourages you to sit on your precious pink rump contemplating your supposed wonderfulness.”

Well, on the one hand, I have often focused on what I lack as an excuse not to act. So I assume that by shifting my focus to what I have in terms of skills, resources, and qualifications, I will also start thinking, "What can I do with this?" Which should lead me to act (differently) and change.

But on the other hand, I can also say that instances of past contentedness has resulted in me sitting on my laurels.

I’M LEANING TOWARDS NO.

3. This affirmation is too broad and not specific enough. ALSO LEANING TOWARDS NO.

4. This affirmation is in the present tense. OK.

5. This affirmation has no negative particles. OK.

6. I can imagine this affirmation being true. OK.

7. N/A

Conclusion: I believe that Law #2 disqualifies this affirmation. When I try to make the affirmation more specific, like “I am grateful for my skills,” it feels too much like self-satisfaction.

Thoughts?

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: About affirmations

You're not sitting on your rump, because using this affirmation means that you've realized that the habit of ingratitude is a problem you have, and you've chosen to fix it.

replies.title: Re: gnosis and other things
replies.content:
1) This is what Zen masters call kensho and the old Western occultists called "the trembling of the Veil" -- the sudden dazzling glimpse of higher realities. It can be an advantage, but it's important not to pursue more experiences of the kind -- let them happen when they happen. I think it was Robert Graves who pointed out that the pursuit of enlightenment experiences can very easily degenerate into an extremely refined form of masturbation!

2) That really varies from person to person. If you find that Slavic occult traditions work for you, great, but you're not required to limit yourself to your cultural or ethnic background.

3) Mixing systems like this can be a very complex matter. If you want to invoke Amaterasu Omikami, why not learn the Shinto way of invoking her, and use that?

4) Yes, you can use those together. Learning Ritual Magic focuses on practical skills, while the Workbook is mostly theory.

5) That's not one I've encountered. Have you seen an eye specialist to make sure it's not something medical?

comments.title: Self and Meaning
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

If the universe (not the various gods and entities that inhabit it but the universe itself) is essentially devoid of personhood and objective meaning, then how did it give rise to entities such as ourselves who are largely defined by our individual personhood and perception of subjective meaning?

JR

replies.title: Re: Self and Meaning
replies.content:
It's an old Greek superstition that something has to be present in a cause if it appears in the effect. That's not true at all -- there are no pancakes in a cup of flour, nor was the theory of relativity present in Einstein's ancestors -- but it still has a weird grip on our collective imagination. Au contraire, given billions of years of time and a universe's worth of matter and energy combining and interacting, everything that's not actually impossible is probably going to pop up somewhere.

There's an interesting refutation of atheism that makes use of that; I first encountered it in one of Gene Wolfe's science fiction novels, of all places. It runs like this: either the universe had a beginning, or it has existed since infinite time. If it had a beginning, then something had to begin it; ergo, at least one god exists. If it has existed from infinite time, in all that vastness of time and space, at least one intelligent being would inevitably have achieved the characteristics we call "divine;" ergo, at least one god exists. QED!

replies.title: Re: Self and Meaning
replies.content:
A very interesting refutation of atheism indeed but I don't think it works by itself. The vast majority of atheists seem to assume materialism, which would limit your intelligent being to technological superiority, not actual divinity.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Self and Meaning
replies.content: Given infinite time, if there's any chance at all that some technology or other would open the way to self-modification resulting in godlike abilities, somebody would do it.

replies.title: Re: Self and Meaning
replies.content:
Well, that just folds the problem into "any chance", doesn't it? Though I suppose that, from a materialist perspective, godlike abilities must be technologically available because there's nothing else.

Incidentally, my comment shows up as (screened comment). As you replied to it, I assume you didn't find it objectionable and simply forgot to put it through?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Self and Meaning
replies.content:
1) That's the sneaky thing about the argument. Most atheists believe that godlike abilities must be technologically available, since that feeds their not very covert anthropolatry. Thus they can't target the obvious weak point in the argument without giving up something they value much more.

2) Not sure what happened; I just re-unscreened it.

replies.title: Re: Self and Meaning
replies.content:
1) Well, there are in fact materialists who do go from the muddled and confusing state of scientific evidence that results from the whole evasive-psi/decline-effect/experimenter-effect/supernatural-plausible-deniability situation, and the various consilient-seeming considerations about the implications that the supernatural would have (e.g. Sean Carroll's effective-field-theory detectability argument, or arguments about religious cosmologies having motte-and-bailey figurativeness and not landing their predictive implications about human nature as squarely as evolutionary psychology does), to an interim judgement in favor of the proposition of materialism, and then from materialism (plus various considerations from cognitive science and physical computer science, e.g. nanomechanical computer proof-of-concept lower bound arguments, reversibility of quantum computation, and the implications of the Church-Turing-Deutsch thesis in re the "algorithmic" nature of intelligence) to the implication that superintelligence and associated technological power are physically possible. They aren't mostly the New Atheist types, because getting to that point requires a certain degree of unconcern about the possibility that, if one fails to allow a mainstream consensus sense of "normality" to stealthily put reins on one's speculative reasoning, that failure might result in one's falling into a pit of (exogenous) social rejection and/or a pit of (endogenous) runaway craziness. New Atheists don't clear that bar. The people who were willing to speculate in such directions instead constitute the extropian/transhumanist/singularitarian/rationality movements/scenes.

But that kind of materialist is also the kind that usually arrives at another interim judgement, in favor of the proposition that physical cause-and-effect is limited by the speed of light. So they do talk about entities having (strictly within-universe/"encosmic") godlike technological abilities, but in terms of like "what would a sphere of superintelligent infrastructure propagating at near the lightspeed limit look like?" and "if its expansion front had passed over us, would we even know, or would it have arranged a 'planetarium' indistinguishable from the cosmos that would have existed in its absence?". This part about a planetarium might sometimes chain into "...or would it for that matter have replaced Earth with a simulation?", although I don't remember concretely any instance of a discussion going that way, as opposed to speculations about simulations being proposed in a way that was more agnostic about why they would have come about.

And of course they also propose the possibility of simulations (which I suppose would be a form of extra-universal/"hypercosmic" godlike technological ability). (The better of them even manage to avoid confused arguments about "probability of being simulated", and instead understand that the relevant question there is more what one's relative priorities should be on having effects on unsimulated versus simulated worlds by one's decisions common to both.) There is also some speculation about "acausal negotiation" that classes of such entities might conduct, by engaging in nested low-resolution representative simulations of each others' originating contexts, and about the equilibria arising from such negotiations. (Despite what you might guess, this speculation has the form of a school of thought developing commonly stipulated plausible premises, and not just elaborating some single pedestalized thinker's sayings. Some of the premises and implications can be seen earlier on in de Chardin and Tipler, and in mainstream philosophy in Adam Elga's 2004 paper "Defeating Dr. Evil with Self-Locating Belief".)

Also, they do catch hell for speculating in these ways, from the parts of the mainstream that are nearby enough to feel social danger from what it would supposedly say about them if they tolerated such speculations. (The main thing that's going on there is a "pre-"-vs.-"trans-" fallacy around the need for the use of social motivations around "scientificness" to restrain other motivational patterns like "credulity" or "exploitativeness" or "psychosis", but it's complicated.)

The reason that they are "materialists" when they grant all those speculative possibilities is, in small part, just a matter of definition. (I think Yudkowsky's position on the concept of "supernatural" could be rendered as, 'if common usage is any guide, "supernatural" refers to a particular illusory ontological possibility of "top-down structureless essence" that human intuition consistently tries to delusively reach for (for predictable reasons, similar to the errors Buddhists correct by teaching anattā), but anything that's made of causally-interacting modular bottom-up parts stands in defiance of the whole structure of that delusive intuition, and is therefore not "supernatural"'.) But mostly, it's a combination of intense fear of being helplessly trapped in individual or collective self-deception, plus the supernatural going far, far out of its way to make it easy to interpret belief in it as having only been caused by one or another abundantly-observable mechanism of individual or collective self-deception, plus forensically interpreting the state of the whole world with a sharper-than-usual sensitivity to predictive subtleties such as "the dog that didn't bark"... adding up to a belief that the claim "the supernatural is real, it's just trying to maintain plausible deniability" is exactly the kind of excuse you would expect to see people making in the world where the supernatural wasn't real. ("So let me get this straight: you want me to believe that your cosmology ate your homework?") This is accompanied by a provisional unwillingness to make any commitments about implications observable from within the headspace associated with that plausible-deniability claim, since it's a premise that might create a sort of liminal "rationality anomie", where it becomes impossible to catch the brain doing the equivalent of runaway accounting fraud but for credence in supernatural beliefs. That leads to an unwillingness to really engage with the topic of "ok but what if the 'supernatural' was real except it was simulators/aliens", other than by glancing acknowledgement that the idea holds together but doesn't seem relevant to our world.

Here are two interesting points where one of these unwillingnesses was substantively overcome:

https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/EgkM9cjunMbYQv3h6/personal-evidence-superstitions-as-rational-beliefs?commentId=GSEqQdaWZmvfRLbLM from 2013: "[...] talking about the ‘supernatural’ in terms of metaphysics is metaphysically interesting but [...] it just clouds the issue unnecessarily. The way most people actually use the concept is just ‘weird things happening that would require human or transhuman agency, in situations where there’s no good reason to suspect human agency’. [...] it doesn’t matter whether the agency comes from an engineered superintelligence or an “ontologically fundamental” god, what matters is there’s non-human agency around. Note that all reports of supernatural phenomena can be explained “naturally” by superintelligences, simulators, highly advanced aliens, &c., all of which seem not-unlikely in a big universe. The improbability stems from the necessity of their having seemingly bizarre motivations; the mechanisms themselves, however, aren’t fantastically improbable." -> the reply "Hew-mons have a long standing tendency to see agency where it does not belong[...] There are conditions (arbitrarily chosen example: prodromal schizophreny …[subtext: the original comment was by someone who'd admitted to having the prodrome for schizophrenia, and who in fact had a pretty bad episode 11 years later -- there appears to have been demonic involvement]) that amplify that bias; from hearing voices to seeing hidden messages." The implied argument there about 'these seem like bizarre motivations to impute' is, from context, something like: why would you simulate something, but then intervene, but then hide those interventions, but only hide them almost-perfectly, instead of hiding them either *completely* perfectly or not at all, and in fact coincidentally in just the right degree to maximally trip up the people on one side of the argument into being wrong?

https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/the-fatima-sun-miracle-much-more and https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/highlights-from-the-comments-on-fatima from October of this year: Scott Alexander isn't dogmatic about materialism. He just finds that, for the most part, the principles of correct and proportionate prediction-checking, as applied to the state of the overall world, favor hypotheses where the supernatural doesn't produce the kinds of scalably-unambiguous observable deviations from materialist expectation that people keep talking about the supernatural as if it would produce, if it existed. These posts walk through the process of how he applies those principles of prediction-checking to the case of the most bewildering exception to that "for the most part". (The second post is a lot shorter, and does a better job of summarizing Alexander's specific points of bafflement, where the observations don't line up with the naive versions of either of the two conflicting hypotheses.)

There's not room in this comment to say much about the competing hypothesized causal mechanism of "anthropolatry", but: Alexander and Yudkowsky both have a deep almost-fearful reverence of the logos or kosmos that governs the accuracy of beliefs formed under incomplete information, and a somewhat more diffuse reverence for the laws of decision theory governing the success of actions taken toward a goal in the face of chance. Yudkowsky additionally has some kind of reverence for a kind of kosmos associated with self-representation in negotiations. (Though not in those words. Among other things, this dramatically inflects how he and other people in the scene write fiction in settings established as containing gods making binding negotiations with each other.) They fear machine superintelligence in large part because they expect that it will conform to these aspects of logos better than humans will, and thereby gain unopposable power. (And, I mean, without being beneficent the way e.g. a Neoplatonist might expect from that description.)

I myself have gotten knocked around enough by conflicting signs and interpretations that I don't really feel that having beliefs about "the supernatural is where the curtain parts enough to let a mind show through somewhere in a top-down idealist cosmology" vs. "the supernatural is caused by a superior technology somewhere in a bottom-up materialist cosmology" is a fit activity for someone at only my level. But I mostly share the positions described above, just taken in a different direction, and with less of a developed coherent reverence and more of a developed paralyzing fear of the seemingly contradictory demands of the relevant aspects of logos. (Though, if the original "real" universe actually was materialist, and this present non-materialist universe was actually a simulation meant in large part just to flesh out and demonstrate the awkward worldbuilding implications of some pareidolic convictions that some people developed in the original "real" universe, then that would explain a number of things.)

(also: I suppose some contentious materialists could call their hypothesized motivational causal mechanism for supernatural belief "pareidolatry")

comments.title: John Lamb Lash, "Not in His Image"
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Are you acquainted with John Lamb Lash and his writings, specifically his popular book "Not in His Image"? In the book he begins by basically tearing apart whatever good reputation the prophetic religions might have by citing examples, such as "the death of Hypatia" in Alexandria, where a mob of Christians murdered this female scholar. Lash argues that until such events, the gnostics were influental pretty much all over Europe at least, but were purged out by Christians, their teachings with too.

Since I know you have extensive interest in the history of ideas, I thought you might have encountered this. If so, did it go down like that, and did the gnostics really worship this feminine entity, Gaia Sophia, the planetary intelligence herself?

replies.title: Re: John Lamb Lash, "Not in His Image"
replies.content:
No, I haven't read his work, and what you've described doesn't make me any more likely to do so. Sophia, the goddess of wisdom worshipped by the Gnostics, is not Gaia, not the planetary intelligence, nor anything like that. If you take some time to read scholarly works about the old Gnostics -- I recommend Bentley Layton's The Gnostic Scriptures, a fine collection with good commentary and annotations -- you'll find that that's a little like claiming that Christians believe that Lucifer, when he was thrown out of heaven, was incarnated as Jesus Christ.

There's a lot of richness in the Gnostic tradition, which has survived right up to the present in some places in the Middle East and elsewhere. Did the orthodox Christians murder a lot of Gnostics? Sure, but the story's much more complex than that.

replies.title: Re: John Lamb Lash, "Not in His Image"
replies.content: I hear a lot of talk about "Archons" around the spiritual scene here, and they feature in Lash's writing as well, as some sort of alien intelligences that spew lies (which resulted in prophetic religions, for instance). Are those Archons real and if so, what are they, if you happen to have a view on them.

replies.title: Re: John Lamb Lash, "Not in His Image"
replies.content:
Okay, this is going to take us down the rabbit hole...

In Hellenistic times, Greek Paganism fused with Mesopotamian astrology to create an astrological theology in which the gods and planets were equated with one another. That became the dominant religious view all through the Mediterranean world, especially among the comfortable classes. Among the poor, the disenfranchised, and the discontented, though, that led to a conviction that the planetary gods were the bad guys of the cosmos, who were responsible for the hard conditions so many people experienced, and were pulling the world year by year into worse and worse conditions.

So you had these underground sects that were convinced that the planetary gods were evil powers, and the real gods were somewhere else, in a world of light to which select human souls could escape. Those were among the traditions that flowed into Gnosticism. They called the planetary powers archons rather than gods -- archon is the Greek word for "ruler, leader." That term became common all through late classical Gnosticism, though not all Gnostic sects believed that the planetary powers were evil -- some held that they were simply ignorant of the true world of light.

So do the archons exist? Of course they do. I prefer to call them planetary intelligences, which is the Renaissance term for them. Are they the villains that Lash wants them to be? Not in my experience.

replies.content:
For what it's worth, I find that the practice of reviewing your day in reverse as you lie in bed recommended in some of our host's books often has the unintended effect of seeing me off to sleep quite effectively. Not much help for getting back to sleep in the middle of the night, though.

One other physical thing I've mentioned here before when folks have asked about insomnia is to consider stopping ingestion of caffeine or other stimulants (like nicotine) much earlier than you might think you need to. I thought I had sleep-onset insomnia for years, then I read that some folks metabolize caffeine much slower than others, and so might have it in their system long after they're consciously aware of any effects. So, I made it a rule not to have caffeinated coffee after 1:00 or 2:00 pm or so, and my sleep improved tremendously after that.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Sleep
replies.content:
About stopping caffeine earlier than you think: same here. I don't drink caffeinated tea after about 2:30pm or so. It may not stop me from falling asleep, but it will keep me awake after I get up to go to the bathroom. (I'm old, these things happen.)

Other things to try: don't drink or eat anything starting a few hours before bedtime - you may need to experiment to determine how many hours is "a few." This is to avoid waking up to go to the bathroom in the middle of the night and then lying awake afterward.

When I wake up in the middle of the night and can't go back to sleep, reading a book on a different topic than whatever I'm thinking about can help. I keep a book with short chapters on something mildly interesting on the nightstand for just such occasions. It works by breaking my attention on whatever I was thinking about. Once I start to feel mildly sleepy I stop reading. It works more than half the time, but not always.

comments.title: Druid concepts into Heathen concepts
comments.content:
This is a request for both JMG and the commentariat- how far off is the following. Thank you in advance for any input.

Strongbear D'Amarbero

This is intended as a sort of “elevator speech”, not a detailed dissertation.

My path includes revival druidry as well as Heathenry; there is more, but these two have been the source of a “side project” I have been working on for a little while. This project is how I would explain some of the druid concepts to a Heathen. Terms such as Abred, Gwynfydd, Ceugant, Awen, Nwyfre, Gwyar, and Calas. As with most terms and concepts related to religion and spirituality, these are nuanced terms that do not lend themselves to direct translations. But I wanted to give it a try. This is what I have so far.

Ask three druids a question and you will get 9 different answers. Is a statement that has proven itself true many times over the years; you could switch out “druid” with “heathen” and lose none of the truth of the statement. However, in this instance, I am referring to concepts attributed to Iolo Morganwg, a Welshman who was influential in the early druid revival.

Lets start with the three levels of existence, Abred, Gwynfydd, and Ceugant.

Abred- the material world of existence, struggle, and experience, known as the "Circle of Necessity". It is the realm where the soul undergoes reincarnation to learn and evolve. It is associated with Matter, the Midworld, and the past.

Probably the easiest answer I could give for this as a heathen is “this is Midgard” because it is the middle world where everything is manifested, although it could be argued that all nine worlds compose the field of existence I would argue more towards Midgard being the closest Heathen term for Abred, but probably also include consideration of wyrd and orlog- the consequences of our actions and choices and the rules or laws of our existence). So, Abred is roughly equivalent to Midgard plus wyrd and orlog.


Gwynfydd- the spiritual realm of perfection, peace, and unity with the divine, achieved when the soul is liberated from the cycle of Abred.

“A hall she sees standing, fairer than the sun,
Thatched with gold, at Gimle;
There the noble fighting-bands will dwell
And enjoy the days of their lives in pleasure”
-Voluspa 61, Larrington translation

This verse, to me, probably is the closest correlation to Gwynfydd since it survives Ragnarok, unlike Valhalla and Sessrumnir, which most people might associate with a paradisiacal type of place.

Ceugant- the unmanifest, unknowable realm of the Godhead, transcending all created existence. It is the ultimate source from which all else emerges.

This is probably the easiest of the three. Before time there was a “yawning gap” between the primordial realms of Muspeheim and Nifelheim- Ginnungagap. The beginning began when the fire and ice met in the middle. So Ceugant is Ginnungagap.

Awen- flowing Spirit, divine inspiration. It is the creative force or "holy breath" that grants wisdom, poetry, and artistic skill.

Is there a Heathen concept that pretty much literally translates into “inspiration, ecstasy, frenzy”? Oðr is that word. Odrerir is the name of the mead of poetry- need I try to find a better word/ term?

Then there are the elements of creation (in a broader creation level sense rather than the familiar air, fire, water, and earth- which “come later”).

Nwyfre is the "Life Force" or "Spirit," which can be found in the Heathen concept of the Soul/Self.

Heathens have fire and ice, which are the elements at the level of Gwyar and Calas.

Gwyar is generally movement, flow, fluidity, and change. The realm of constant movement is Muspelheim, the realm of fire that has been here since the beginning.

Calas is the form that allows life to exist in a secure, stable, and predictable manner. The realm of solidity and stasis is Nifelheim, the realm of ice that has been here since the beginning.

replies.title: Re: Druid concepts into Heathen concepts
replies.content:

Gwynfydd is the realm of beings who are in spiritual bodies, and Ceugant is the realm of the Absolute Divine." I think most Heathens would get that!

replies.title: Re: Druid concepts into Heathen concepts
replies.content:
Well, this is certainly very interesting, if nothing else! As a druid and a Heathen myself, this kind of comparison is often on my mind, though at least so far, I have not found direct one-to-one translation to be especially fruitful, at least if treated as an exact correspondence. Rather, what has been helpful for me is to see both what is in common between the two traditions, but also where they have different perspectives and ways of looking at things (a process I also do between Hermetic concepts and Heathen ones).

Btw, if you haven't checked out Winifred Hodge Rose's work, I strongly suggest you check it out, especially for a very different perspective on the soul(s) and a way of understanding subtle bodies.

replies.title: Re: Druid concepts into Heathen concepts
replies.content:
I can see some point to what you are trying to do, Strongbear, and / but also agree with JMG and Jeff's comments. It's an interesting intellectual endeavor to try to translate concepts from one cultural / linguistic framework into another, but to me the end result tends to illustrate their differences more clearly than it does their similarities, with some caveats.

One caveat is that it depends on how deep one wants to go. If one wants the 5-minute version then I'd say what you're proposing here works to an extent, Strongbear. But the deeper one goes, the more clear the differences become. This is much of what I've been working on doing for several decades now: 'translate' philosophical and spiritual concepts that come from very different, ancient Heathen worldviews and languages into something modern English-speaking Heathens can begin to get an inkling of, beginning of course with myself.

What you're offering is like a dictionary entry: "translate Druid terms into Heathen ones." That can be useful for ecumenical discussions and the like, I would think. But I know from my own decades of study and experience that dictionary-level translations are quite superficial when it comes to deep concepts, and in fact can be misleading when the differences between the full meanings of the original term versus the word in translation are actually greater than their similarities. For example, my efforts to find an old Germanic Heathen word for 'soul' turned into two decades of work and a 1000-page two-volume tome on Heathen soul lore, and I've recently published another tome on the concept of 'orlog'. There are many spiritual and philosophical concepts that are best understood within their own cultural frameworks, and the more rounds of linguistic and cultural translation they go through, the more their meanings are confused.

Now, I think it would be quite interesting intellectually to do an in-depth comparison between the Druid concepts you list, and the comparable Heathen terms you list with them. Keep in mind the 'translations' that would have to be done here: from one language family to another (Germanic languages to Celtic languages), from one time period to another: from Proto-Germanic time period into the early Middle Ages, say roughly 500 BCE to 1300 CE, compared to wherever and whenever Iolo Morganwg's source material came from. Different cultures, different time periods, different languages. Then whatever we extract from all that is expressed and described in modern English, going through another profound translation process and conceptual shaping.

To me, it's enough of a challenge to translate old Heathen concepts, and arcane Druid concepts, into modern English language and concepts. Trying to translate them into each others' concepts using modern English as the bridge between the two poses even more of a challenge! But that applies to the in-depth version, and people might understandably roll their eyes and say "I just wanted the 5-minute version, thank you!" when offered these complexities! In that case, your outline could be quite useful, I would think, except I agree with JMG that the differences between Ceugant and Ginnungagap are considerably greater than their similarities.

In frith,
Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.content:
Hey JMG,

1) Sigils have been stuck on my mind, which is perhaps appropriate for such a "sticky" subject. This week, I'm wondering whether there is a meaningful difference between affirmations and sigils.

In my own experiments with sigils, before I understood that I don't know what I'm doing, I largely tried to affect change in the world, with predictable results: enough effects to prove that something happened, not enough to actually make a difference.

Contrasting this with Gordon White's advice (who, incidentally, has a tasteful activation method that I'm partial to in his Chaos Protocols, that includes a banishing, prayer, and then staring each sigil until it feels "active"), who recommends that one first does shadow work to actually become clear on what one really wants and what's preventing that and then moves on to sigils that effect inner change, I'm reminded of your own advice, which more often than not seems to boil down to "journal to figure out what you want and why you're not getting it, then use an affirmation to change yourself into someone who does get that."

Would it be fair to say that sigils and affirmations are largely analogous?

2) Last week, I was at a (metal) concert. About halfway through, I started feeling… weird. I'm not sure if it was unpleasant but it certainly wasn't pleasant either. My body moved pretty much automatically, which isn't unusual by itself: I've probably become aware of this at about half the concerts I've attended so far (most of them sober, I should perhaps add). When this happens, the music fades into the background and I withdraw into thoughts. This usually isn't at all unpleasant but after a minute or so, I realise that this quite defeats the purpose of going to a concert and return to being present.

This time, though, I noticed that I wasn't really reacting to the music but to the crowd. I clapped along with them and made the appropriate rhythmic movements but it felt as though I was missing some impulse and I also spent some while just staring off into space unmovingly. This is the second time this happened. The first time led straight into a panic attack; this time, it was just mildly interesting, perhaps because I was already familiar with such a situation, perhaps because I was completely sober (unlike the first time, though I only had two or three beers then), or perhaps simply because friends were close by.

You've mentioned before that concerts involve energy movements from the audience to the band and, at least at good concerts, back from the band to the audience. Given that my friends didn't really think the concert was better than alright either, could this be what I felt?

—David P.

comments.title: Ars Al-Mandal
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This might not be quite in your wheelhouse but I thought I'd try asking you.

I have a Buddhist background but I've always been interested in Renaissance-era ritual magic. I read a lot about the different grimoires.

One interesting grimoire that I think hasn't attracted as much attention as the flashy demon-summoning ones is the Almadel/Almandal.

Based on the name alone, and some surviving Arabic traditions, it is quite obvious that this comes from Sanskrit "mandala" through an Arabic medium (Al-Mandal). A few academic sources actually state that this is a specifically Buddhist use of the word in a ritual context, since Brahmanical sources never use the word "mandala" in this sense, they use the word "yantra" instead.

European magicians adapted it to their own beliefs and practices of course, but it seems that it has retained its more "theurgical" character throughout as opposed to the many different Key of Solomon-derivatives which feature aerial spirits or demons.

I also practise Tibetan Vajrayana, where there is no physical object mandala used in the same way as the Almandel, but the symbolism of entering into the mandala in an initiation, and also visualizing it in different practices is quite common.

For quite a while, I've been musing on the idea of adapting the Almadel ritual structure, with the brass tablet, back into a Buddhist framework. I'd start with the version in the Lemegeton with 4 seasons and sets of angels, but substitute the Buddhas of the Five Directions (Four Cardinal Directions + Center), and adapt invocations from different sutras.

What do you think of this idea? What do you think I should be on the look out for if I decide to proceed?

replies.title: Re: Ars Al-Mandal
replies.content: I've worked with the wax almadels described in the Ars Almadel, as one of several branches of angel magic. I suppose it could be re-reworked to fit a Buddhist framework, though -- as with all such things -- the only way to find out whether it works is to do it. Be sure you have the necessary chops to banish evil spirits before you begin.

replies.title: Re: Ars Al-Mandal
replies.content:
Some of the Medieval Latin texts on using an almandal were published by Julien Véronèse in 2012, L'Almandal et l'Almadel latins au Moyen Âge: Introduction et éditions critiques (Micrologus' Library, Salomon Latinus, vol. 2). Véronèse does excellent scholarly work on Medieval magic, so this volume is basic for the history of almandal work.

On the practical side, the American ceremonial magician Poke Runyon (Frater Thabion) has put out a fascinating DVD called The Magick of Solomon that shows some of his own magical work using a wax almandal/almadel.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi John,


On your other blog you wrote this in relation to psychic self-defense:

"Flashy and fascinating as they are, magical workings are weaker than the other option, which is the way of self-development. Meditation rather than ritual is central to this second option, and its goal is to strengthen and focus consciousness so that the practitioner doesn’t need ritual workings to interact with the web of connections linking mind to mind. It’s a commonplace of older occult literature that an adept—a genuinely skilled practitioner of magic—can accomplish his will with minimal rituals, or none at all. You don’t get to that level by doing rituals; you get there by the dull but essential discipline of daily practice."

Q1. Does this mean that if one were to practice diligently with New Thought methods that one could get the same results as with ritual? Or are you only referring to psychic self-defense? I think there might be a difference, but I could be wrong.


Q2. A lot of modern New Thought methods don't use mind training like the older material recommended. With sufficient training in discursive meditation, concentration, relaxation and other techniques to help control one's thoughts and subconscious thought patterns, what are the upper limits of New Thought practices or other methods that don't rely on ritual?


Thanks in advance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You won't get all the same benefits, since daily ritual practice develops skills that New Thought methods don't, and those skills remain with you even if you don't need rituals to use them. But you can go very far.

2) Nobody knows. Nobody knows the upper limits of ritual magic, for that matter. Certainly you can accomplish a very great deal either way.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One of the early New Thought authors, William Walker Atkinson, wrote a substantial work about the practice of what he called Mental Magic. In its earliest version, it appeared as two separate volumes, (1) The Secret of Mental Magic: A Course of Seven Lessons and (2) Mental Fascination, both published in 1907. In the next year (1908) he combined them into a single, larger volume, Mind Power; or, The Law of Dynamic Mentation, and then in 1912 he published a slightly revised edition, Mind Power: The Secret of Mental Magic. I think the system he lays out here is generally as effective as systems of ritual magic.

Before Atkinson, Charles Godfrey Leland devised a quite similar, and similarly effective system of practice, which became most widely available in his book, The Mystic Will (1907). (Earlier editions of his book, from 1899 onward, were oddly titled Have You a Strong Will?) This is the same Charles G. Leland who in the same year (1899) also published Aradia; or, The Gospel of the Witches of Italy, one of the foundation texts of 20th-century Wicca!

comments.title: Rambling request for additional insight
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thanks for hosting this as always.

I took up magic in the Golden Dawn tradition through some offshoot groups and teachers at some point, the result of which led me to pursue Christianity. I became baptized and confirmed in the Episcopal church.

My life fell apart and I switched to the path of Hearth-Fire, having to give up a very successful career of 15 years, obsolesced by technological changes.

I spent several years focusing only on rebuilding from the ground up, studying a new trade, downsizing, moving across the country, and becoming financially stable. Throughout, I haven’t entirely given up the things I’ve learned. I sometimes pray (to Christian and pagan deities) and perform divination, for myself and others. But I’m considering the wealth of options at hand to begin working towards spiritual self-transmutation again.

I have a bit of an attraction to Thelema and have a couple friends who it clearly works for and are exemplary people but was disappointed by the OTO, which felt like a LARP club, and I don’t care about so-called “sex magick.” I feel at home with Christianity, but the most powerful parishes are too conservative for me and the more liberal churches are too liberal and barely spiritual. I’ve had experiences with Egyptian deities, especially Thoth, but I don’t feel like I know how to, or should be, pursuing a Kemetic spirituality. My prayers and divinations along these lines haven’t yielded clear answers.

I’ve done a lot of meditating and journaling and divination about all of this, here and there, across years, and it all feels like going in circles. The main pursuit, aside from the rebuild project, has been tons and tons of reading, which I even do in off-time while at work. I have the feeling I’ve been around a while and I’m ready to get off this wheel, and reading only goes so far, and I’m called to be active in moving on. I’ve been encouraged by friends to become a Mason and was introduced to someone running a local Martinist group, but I don’t know if the time commitment, alongside my work schedule and family commitments, are worth the effort.

I guess one way I could frame this, is does it make more sense for me to pick Martinism, Masonry, Christianity, some combination, or just continue the path of Hearth-Fire and deal with karma in a smooth and subtle day to day way? I’m wondering if you have any thoughts, given my background, what the best use of my time is? I’m guessing you might say essentially that everyone is different and only I can answer that question, and that maybe it’s best to simply choose and take any action and not look back. If that’s the case, that’s very understandable. But I’m curious if you have anything to say that might be something I haven’t thought of, before I choose to either do nothing but focus on my job and family, or, say, join a Martinist group, or just pick up where I left off working solo through the Golden Dawn system…

Thank you and blessings to you if you will have them.

replies.title: Re: Rambling request for additional insight
replies.content: What I'd recommend is that no matter what else you do, you maintain (if you have one) or establish (if you don't) a daily practice of prayer and meditation. That's where the rubber meets the road; all groups, all churches, all lodges, and all teachings are secondary to that kind of simple but relentless basic practice. Alongside that -- why, then it really is up to you. There is no one right answer for everyone, and it's quite possible that there's more than one right answer to you -- but in any case, establishing or maintaining those basic practices will give you a foundation on which you can build, and help clear your mind and heart as well.

comments.title: Cursed Dynasty?
comments.content:
Delightful meme, JMG!

I have a question that is rather ‘out there’ but I can’t think of anyone better able to answer it than you. It may seem rather silly, but it has persisted in my mind for years. My question is whether families or dynasties can be cursed. What prompts this question is the fate of the Stewart monarchs during a period of nearly 250 years (1406 – 1649). Let’s take a walk along memory lane:

- James I: assassinated in his castle’s sewer in 1437 at the age of 42

- James II: killed while standing beside his favourite cannon (which exploded) in 1460 at the age of 29

- James III: killed by a rebel pretending to be a priest in 1488 at the age of 36

- James IV: killed in a battle with the English at Flodden Field, Northumberland in 1513 at the age of 40
(immortalized by the song ‘Flowers of the Forest’ played on the bagpipes at every November 11 Remembrance Day in Canada)

- James V: died three weeks after his army’s defeat by his uncle King Henry VIII’s army at Solway Moss in 1542, at the age of 30

- Mary I: executed by her cousin Elizabeth I in 1587 at the age of 40

- James VI (James I of England): beat the odds/curse by dying in his bed in 1625 at the age of 58 and leaving behind an adult heir

- Charles I of Scotland and England: executed as a result of England’s Second Civil War in 1649 at the age of 48

Of course, it was not particularly common during the time for the monarchs of nearly any European nation to die peacefully at a ripe old age (their lives were more typically ‘nasty, brutish and short’ – consider it to be an occupational hazard) – but still: for century after century you had a Stewart infant/toddler coronated, ruled for maybe a decade after gaining the age of majority and died violently leaving an infant heir to wait for becoming the age of majority. Rinse and repeat ad nauseum. Are dynastic curses possible, or are we to attribute this saga of the Stewart monarchs to just a run of extremely bad luck combined with the misfortune of ruling a kingdom next-door to the aggressive Anglo-Saxons?

And to “give” to the community – in case anyone has an interest in mysticism, I have recently started a weekly blog in which I post my musings based on my personal perspectives and experiences as a life-long mystic and invite others to share their perspectives: https://mystical-mountain-9.dreamwidth.org/. All are welcome!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Cursed Dynasty?
replies.content:
Oh, it's not just bad luck. Scottish politics were savage and bloodsoaked even by the standards of the Middle Ages, and it was very much to the advantage of powerful Scots lairds to see to it that no adult monarch lasted long. Nor did James VI and I just beat the odds, he beat the Scots nobility into a pulp. He was a geeky, socially awkward, intellectually gifted guy -- everything about him screams "autism" to me -- who became extremely good at getting people to underestimate him, and then crushing them.

Oh, and you should have added the other Stuarts! Charles II inherited some of his grandfather's brains, and succeeded in getting himself restored to the throne his father had lost, dodging any number of murderous conspiracies, and dying in his bed. His brother James II didn't have the same gifts, and got driven from the throne in 1688. James II's son, the Old Pretender James II, lived a long and useless life, scheming unsuccessfully to retake the British throne; his son was Bonnie Prince Charlie, who was brave, handsome, charismatic, idealistic, and clueless, and led his followers to a crushing and final defeat at Culloden, after which he did the smart thing and drank himself to death.

I don't know if there was actually a curse involved, but yeah, the Stuarts were a hot mess.

replies.title: Re: Disappearing Building
replies.content:
Skipping over natural explanations, the easiest-to-rattle-off supernatural explanations are something like:

- The demolished version of the lot represents a possibility or a historical memory that had had some weight or significance to it, and it impressed itself on your mind by a full-on replacement phantasm. (That makes the demolished version sound like it's in a separate, less-real category than the shop building, but there's a case to be made that everything in our material world is just an especially coherent and reliable kind of phantasm impressing itself in the same kind of way, so that this would be a large distinction between degrees but not a distinction between kinds.) I don't know why it would have done so, though.

- Maybe that detail of the demolition timing hadn't been pinned down to a solid consensus, by the point at which that part of your subjective stream of remembered experiences was getting pinned down. While normally it's a high priority to do things like maintaining different versions of your memories corresponding to different external details so that either way it could all be consistent in retrospect from further in the future, this time for some reason the priority was low instead. (E.g. if there are reasons people wouldn't believe you compared to other people, then your testimony constitutes less of a "loose end", and maybe the experience is important or something.)

- As above, but the revision was on a knife-edge where it could be swayed by your opinions about condos and other development options. In that case, if your opinion is consequential that way, then leaving land unused causes a little bit of economic strain, so you might want to think through your standards for what you'd welcome seeing, even just a non-eyesore condo.

replies.title: Re: Neoplatonic worship
replies.content: You might consider the very traditional habit of praying to the planetary god of each day of the week, using the Orphic Hymn to that god. That's become a very common prayer practice among occultists, and it's part of what I do on a daily basis. You light some incense as an offering, turn your attention to the planetary deity, and recite the Orphic hymn of the deity as a prayer. (You can find the Orphic Hymns quite readily online.) It's quite simple but it can be very profound.

I assume you have no explanation for this and the prescription will likely be to meditate on this further.

comments.title: A weird question

One more question, if I may.

In my history of dating, I’ve been with women who, at various times, have suddenly, seemingly inexplicably, started bleeding from the gums. In one instance it happened regularly and I asked if she had messed with anything occult, like an ouija board. She said she was given a crystal ball and also felt a presence on her shoulder for many years, and I asked if the crystal ball was in our house and she said yes. At the time I wasn’t religious and wasn’t an occultist. I had a weird feeling and listened to this.

The feeling led me to ask her to take the crystal ball and get in the car with me. I didn’t know why. She agreed. I started driving. I got a feeling to turn on a street. Then to turn again on another street. I got a feeling to stop the car and to say this to her: “take the crystal ball and go to that one exact trash bin up the street (that I pointed to) and put it into that bin.” I pointed not at the nearest one to us but another specific one further up. She got out and did it and came back spooked. She said the bin I pointed out, unlike the rest of the bins, had a square cut out of the lid the exact size of the box with the crystal ball so she didn’t have to open the lid but dropped it into the cut out.

She didn’t bleed anymore.

More recently, with my current partner, this happened more than a year ago. We moved across the country and it didn’t happen anymore. Then we went to Las Vegas and had a few creepy experiences. Now, back in town, it happened to her again.

I’m thinking spiritual protective practices are in order, but my question is: do you know of this in any traditional lore? Or have any ideas about the cause or what this typically represents? I’ve never seen blood pouring from the mouth in any accounts I’ve ever encountered and I’ve asked occultists around me, who similarly have no idea what I’m talking about.

I’m wary of immediately jumping to “demons” and I’m open to ideas, from nonspiritual coincidence to self-induced spiritual “blocks” or other potential ideas, or even me being a common factor.

Thanks for hosting this and blessings if you’ll have them.

comments.title: Cleansing my home
comments.content:
I have a fascinating account to share. So I recently decided to cleanse my house of all old negative energy. I recently bought it out from my ex partner/baby momma and found that it just seemed like her aura was hovering around - the house is always a mess, everyone is walking on eggshells and ready to fight despite the fact our tormenter is gone... it was time to cleanse everything and start anew.

I had obtained a couple of sage sticks, and planned to fully sage the house, then place salt in 4 jars, get 4 nails, bless the salt and the nails and put a bent nail in each jar, adding sage ash to make black salt. I intended to incorporate this into a sphere of protection ritual where I impressed protective and cleansing energy into each charm while facing each direction, then visualized a sphere expanding to encompass my home, pushing all unwanted energy out, and then placing the charm in each corner of the house.

That was the plan. It got derailed almost immediately. I had requested that everyone be out of the house for this, to the point I gave my step daughter, my ex's daughter, my truck and some money to go out and have fun. She doesn't like the smell of sage which had significantly delayed me from doing this.

I lit the sage and then went from room to room, shaking the energy loose by banging pots and pans and calling forth the energies of my ex and all old occupants, demanding they leave this place. Then I came to my atepdaughter's room. She had locked it. Which meant I couldn't sage her room, do the southern direction of the sphere of protection, or place the charm in that corner.

I came outside as she was about to leave. I'm usually pretty capm, especially with her after everything her mom put her through, but I was filled with this... demanding, irrational rage. This insistence that I had better get my way. I was mean, controlling, petulant. She asked me to respect her space.

I went back inside and was just getting more and more worked up. I placed the sage sticks by the input vent of the hvac system and turned it to fan on - which I discovered, creates enough air to really burn uo the sage and really does effectively get it throughout the house! But I wasn't satisfied - I was being filled with more and more rage. I started sending a string of nasty texts, making elaborate threats, becoming completely unhinged and derailed - something I never do.

Then my stepdaughter stopped everything with 2 words. "Hi, Jamie." Jamie is her mother's name. I stopped dead in the runaway train of anger and thoughts. I looked at myself in the mirror and realized I HAD BECOME POSSESED BY THE VERY SPIRIT I WAS TRYING TO DRIVE OUT!

It was like I suddenly saw her in the mirror. All her demanding, compulsive behaviors , the belief that the ends of getting her way justified cruel means... there it was, staring me in the face. I suddenly felt like I had to get out. I saw her in the swirling smoke, her angry visage ina. thousand fights... and more. I saw what looked like snaggle toothed skeletons cooking something nefarious in the kitchen, visions of acts from the past, playing themselves out again and again in the house.

I opened the back door and stumbled outside. I sat down and took some deep breaths. I looked back and saw smoke curling out of the house, and with it the writhing of the figures I had seen inside. I sat and reflected on what had just happened.

A few reflections. First, be very careful when trying to remove any sort of spirit or energy - you definitely can come to be possessed, be filled with its energy, and derailed from your purpose. And I had done a sphere of protection before doing this! But I'm glad it haooened, because it hammered home the true lesson - if I wanted her energy gone, first and foremost it had to start with me. I had to refuse to ever embody that mindset, that train of thoughts and emotions that justify demanding your way with no concern for others.

I started by profusely apologizing, openly admitting I had acted just like her mother, amd swearing it would never happen again. I made an oath to Jupiter to never behave like this again, and then called on him to drive the darkness out - first out of me, then my home. I had already opened the windows but placing sage by the hvac input vent really works - the house was completely filled with smoke. I pulled the sage away from the vent and opened both doors, got fans going, sprinkled black salt everywhere, and then began the ritual.

I had to modify it a bit - I did it in the center of the house, facing each direction, but that was better in my opinion - less running around rewuired so I could focus on visualizing the sphere encompassing the house. As for how to get the charms into each corner of the house - I went under the house and placed them upon the support beams at each corner of the foundation. That actually seemed like an improvement. At the end, I felt at peace.

It was right after that my stepdaighter's boyfriend came home and wanted to talk. I apologized for evreything, let him know it was all fine, and laughed at the idea of becoming what you want to be rid of. He actually opened up her room and told me if I really wanted to sgae it I could. I said I kind of already had but appreciated the gesture. I went in, did a wuick sweep, and then told him to open the window so it wouldnt smell like sage. We kept the eindows open and fans going and chatted psychology, spirituality, reincarnation, and philosophy. When my stepdaughter came home, I made sure she knew everything was fine, and I would always respect her and her space. She was grateful but said it was really surreal watching me turn into her mom - she said I had her mannerisms and everything.

So, make of the tale what you will. For my part, I'm taking away that any change in energy starts within, and if you haven't cleansed your own soul of the energy then when you try to use workings to drive it out you can actually invite it in - though that can help you if it holds up a mirror!

comments.title: Nothing happens by accident
comments.content: No question this week but something odd to share. I had purchased a tin cup from Tucker Carlson’s merch shop last year. It has a graphic of Trump, his ear bleeding and his fist raised with an American flag behind him, on the reverse, a quote from Tucker, A Leader is the Bravest Man. When the latest documentary about Thomas Crooks released this week, I put it on as I was cleaning some dishes. Although I have zero recollection of hitting the cup, when I picked it up to wash it, I noticed a significant chip directly under the Trump graphic. The cup is white, and the chip looks like a creepy inverted eye. It had been a favorite cup this past year and I don’t intend to get rid of it due to the damage but I find it’s occurrence and the timing of it odd in a way that only an occultist could appreciate or make sense of.

replies.content: Odd indeed!
